title: TLS 1.3, Certificate Identity, ALPN, and HTTPS · Runtime Recall
summary: A delayed handoff reconstructs tls 1.3, certificate identity, alpn, and https from bounded byte and state evidence, challenges one misconception, and revises the TLS identity, negotiation, and protocol boundary record. Planned artifact: Extend the evidence record for TLS identity, negotiation, and protocol boundary record after a delayed changed case. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Distinguish TCP connection, TLS records, handshake, authenticated peer identity, traffic keys, alerts, application data, and closure.
Validate certificate chain, time, DNS or IP reference identity, key usage, revocation policy, and trust roots without insecure skip flags.
Offer and select application protocols with ALPN before application data and reject unsupported or inconsistent negotiation.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Tls Record Handshake Boundary · changed case · Review Tls Record Handshake Boundary
steps.instruction:
review case — A review protocol team handles case 9524 in a incident replay lab retaining minimized request-smuggling corpora. Build deterministic Go evidence to gate HTTPS on certificate identity, TLS policy, ALPN, and explicit early-data review; preserve this operating constraint: no malformed framing may be normalized and forwarded to another recipient; then append a second pipelined message and prove the first parser leaves the exact residual bytes. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Competency probe: State that TLS protects a connection hop but does not provide application authorization, safe methods, trusted intermediaries, malware safety, or end-to-end storage secrecy.

For Record what required effort · Review Https Non Guarantees, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, failure, race, or toolchain evidence: HTTPS alone makes every request authorized and every received payload safe.
